title: From Ease To Difficulty
study: A Study In The Life Of Joseph
scriptureForWeek: GENESIS 45:9-15 “Now hurry back to my father and tell him, ‘This is what your son Joseph says: God has made me master over all the land of Egypt. So come down to me immediately! You can live in the region of Goshen, where you can be near me with all your children and grandchildren, your flocks and herds, and everything you own. I will take care of you there, for there are still five years of famine ahead of us. Otherwise you, your household, and all your animals will starve.’” Then Joseph added, “Look! You can see for yourselves, and so can my brother Benjamin, that I really am Joseph! Go tell my father of my honored position here in Egypt. Describe for him everything you have seen, and then bring my father here quickly.” Weeping with joy, he embraced Benjamin, and Benjamin did the same. Then Joseph kissed each of his brothers and wept over them, and after that they began talking freely with him. * This week has long sections of scripture that are several chapters. These verses above set the stage for the confrontation and reconciliation that will happen. For the longer sections of the context and story, refer to the readings at the top of each day.
weekTheme: Boys cling, men adjust The teenage years are defined by change. Educational changes, physical changes, changes in friendships, changes in faith, changes in interests and passions. Yet in the midst of all these changes there can be anxiety and angst. Being a teenager often feels like being caught between 2 worlds: adulthood and adolescence. Sometimes guys can cling to the safety of the past and miss out on what God wants to do in the future. Learning to accept change and embrace what comes is one of the keys to mastering manhood. Joseph's life will end up looking nothing like he dreamed or imagined, but he accepted God's will and leaned into his purpose. This week, we will discuss the importance of embracing change and developing agility.

days.title: The Difficulty Of Change
days.scripture: GENESIS 45:9-15 9 “Now hurry back to my father and tell him, ‘This is what your son Joseph says: God has made me master over all the land of Egypt. So come down to me immediately! 10 You can live in the region of Goshen, where you can be near me with all your children and grandchildren, your flocks and herds, and everything you own. 11 I will take care of you there, for there are still five years of famine ahead of us. Otherwise you, your household, and all your animals will starve.’” 12 Then Joseph added, “Look! You can see for yourselves, and so can my brother Benjamin, that I really am Joseph! 13 Go tell my father of my honored position here in Egypt. Describe for him everything you have seen, and then bring my father here quickly.” 14 Weeping with joy, he embraced Benjamin, and Benjamin did the same. 15 Then Joseph kissed each of his brothers and wept over them, and after that they began talking freely with him.
days.reflection: Are you good or bad at accepting change? Some guys love change. They are excited about new things and embrace whatever comes their way. Others hate change. They prefer routine and structure and see change as a threat to the things they have come to love. Which one do you identify with more? One of the hardest things about growing up and becoming a man is the amount of change that you have to handle. Dealing with the difficulty of change is something you are going to need to embrace. You will either be dragged into change against your will, or embrace change as a part of life and learn to navigate it well. Either way, change is coming. Joseph's whole family is going to be rocked by changes. Joseph's dad, Jacob, is going to live out his final years as a stranger in another country. And then Jacob is going to experience the protection and provision of a son he thought was dead. All of Joseph's brothers are going to have to relocate their whole lives and families to Egypt as well. All they have known and all their families have known will be taken away and replaced with something else. They will have to learn to adjust to new customs, a new language, new religious traditions, new food, and new neighbors. And they are going to have to do this in the middle of a crisis. You are going to be rocked by changes too. Changes in your body, changes in your friends, changes in schools and classes, and changes you don’t see coming. When change happens, guys can tend to react in two extreme ways. First, they can turn into control freaks and try and take charge. Or two, they can try and escape the changes by pretending nothing is happening and burying their head in the sand. Even though these can be natural reactions, there is a healthier way to respond. Change psychologists tell us that dealing with change can be like riding a wave. Our reactions can go through 4 movements: 1-Shock and freaking out. 2-Anger and resistance. 3-Coming to terms with the "new normal." 4-Acceptance, adjusting, moving forward. Joseph had to go through all these stages throughout his life. His family is about to experience these stages when they move to Egypt. And if you haven’t already, you will soon. Change is natural, inevitable, and hard, but you can get through it. It really helps to know what stage you are in too. That way you can manage your emotions and begin to lean into where God is leading you. And that can really take away the anxiety and provide some stable hope.
questions: Do you think you find it easy to change? Why or why not? Can you give an example? What is one of the hardest changes you have had to face in your life so far? What made it so hard? Is there any change you are struggling with right now? Ask the person you are doing this study with to share a time they went through a hard change and how they experienced all 4 stages of change.

thoughts.verse: Peace I leave with you; my peace I give you. I do not give to you as the world gives. Do not let your hearts be troubled and do not be afraid.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to help you with any changes you are currently struggling with. Ask God to steady your heart and give you peace.

days.title: Being Present
days.scripture: GENESIS 46:28-34 28 As they neared their destination, Jacob sent Judah ahead to meet Joseph and get directions to the region of Goshen. And when they finally arrived there, 29 Joseph prepared his chariot and traveled to Goshen to meet his father, Jacob. When Joseph arrived, he embraced his father and wept, holding him for a long time. 30 Finally, Jacob said to Joseph, “Now I am ready to die, since I have seen your face again and know you are still alive.” 31 And Joseph said to his brothers and to his father’s entire family, “I will go to Pharaoh and tell him, ‘My brothers and my father’s entire family have come to me from the land of Canaan. 32 These men are shepherds, and they raise livestock. They have brought with them their flocks and herds and everything they own.’” 33 Then he said, “When Pharaoh calls for you and asks you about your occupation, 34 you must tell him, ‘We, your servants, have raised livestock all our lives, as our ancestors have always done.’ When you tell him this, he will let you live here in the region of Goshen, for the Egyptians despise shepherds.”
days.reflection: If you could live at any point in time, what would it be? Would you go back in time to the good old days? Or into the future with incredibly cool technology? Time travel is one of those things that people always seem to dream about. There are so many movies about the possibility of cruising through time and undoing mistakes or knowing the lottery numbers and changing our future. However, we can’t. We are creatures trapped in time, stuck having to live day by day, moment by moment. And this is an important reality to accept. Because if we get freaked out by change, we can miss the good work God wants to do in the moment. We can dream about going back or trying to escape into the future without seeing the opportunities and purpose of the change he is bringing us. Joseph and his family have dealt with a staggering amount of change over the course of their lives. And yet, in the midst of the pain, struggle, and transition, beautiful moments are waiting to happen, because they are paying attention. After over 20 years apart, Joseph and his father, the one who loved him and gave him the favored coat, are going to be reunited. It’s a really moving scene. Joseph prepared his chariot and traveled to Goshen to meet his father, Jacob. When Joseph arrived, he embraced his father and wept, holding him for a long time. Finally, Jacob said to Joseph, “Now I am ready to die, since I have seen your face again and know you are still alive.” This reunion took place in the midst of a famine, migration, and major dislocation. It’s a powerful reminder that in the midst of change and even chaos, God is working out his plans for our good if we can pay attention and see it. That’s why we have to be present and face change head-on. Because the best place to encounter God's goodness is in the moment. Jesus said in Matthew 6:25-27 “That is why I tell you not to worry about everyday life — whether you have enough food and drink, or enough clothes to wear. Isn’t life more than food, and your body more than clothing? Look at the birds. They don’t plant or harvest or store food in barns, for your heavenly Father feeds them. And aren’t you far more valuable to him than they are? Can all your worries add a single moment to your life?" God can meet you in the middle of the hard changes in your life too. He can meet you in your exams when you are stressed. He can meet you in your friendships when conflicts emerge. He is always there, in the moment, with you and for you.
questions: Do your thoughts tend to drift toward the past or the future? Do you find yourself daydreaming about being older or younger?
Why do you think people your age struggle with change so much? What do you find most stressful about it?

Ask the person you are doing this study with to share a time they noticed something cool happening in the middle of a season of change. Ask them how they learned to pay attention and be present in the midst of all the movement.

thoughts.verse: "For I know the plans I have for you,” declares the Lord, “plans to prosper you and not to harm you, plans to give you hope and a future.”
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to help you be more present. Ask him to help you see what he is building in you and doing around you when you are going through change.

days.title: God Will Guide Us Through Change
days.scripture: GENESIS 46:1-7 1 So Jacob set out for Egypt with all his possessions. And when he came to Beersheba, he offered sacrifices to the God of his father, Isaac. 2 During the night God spoke to him in a vision. “Jacob! Jacob!” he called. “Here I am,” Jacob replied. 3 “I am God, the God of your father,” the voice said. “Do not be afraid to go down to Egypt, for there I will make your family into a great nation. 4 I will go with you down to Egypt, and I will bring you back again. You will die in Egypt, but Joseph will be with you to close your eyes.” 5 So Jacob left Beersheba, and his sons took him to Egypt. They carried him and their little ones and their wives in the wagons Pharaoh had provided for them. 6 They also took all their livestock and all the personal belongings they had acquired in the land of Canaan. So Jacob and his entire family went to Egypt — 7 sons and grandsons, daughters and granddaughters — all his descendants.
days.reflection: The hardest thing about change is the uncertainty of knowing what will happen. It’s not knowing how things are going to turn out that creates stress. If you are not a follower of Jesus, change can be terrifying because you have no one to lead you or assure you that the change will work out for your good. But as a Christian, God promises to guide us through change into a future that honors him and fills us with joy. From middle school to middle age and high school to retirement, you can face the future with confidence because you don’t have to walk into it alone. God himself promises to walk with us. Change can be scary, but we won’t be alone. We will have a guide. Even though Joseph struggled with the changes forced on him when he was taken to Egypt, he ultimately saw that it was God who was working to fulfill his dreams and destiny. Remember the answer he gave his brothers when he was reunited with them in Egypt? Genesis 45:5, “But don’t be upset, and don’t be angry with yourselves for selling me to this place. It was God who sent me here ahead of you to preserve your lives.” Though it was terrifying on one level to go through what he went through, God led and guided him. It says that God was with him and showed him favor in Potiphar’s house. God was with him and showed him favor in prison. Even though the changes were hard, he had the comfort of God's presence. And now God is going to do the same thing for Joseph's father, Jacob. God appears to Jacob with these incredibly comforting words. “I am God, the God of your father,” the voice said. “Do not be afraid to go down to Egypt, for there I will make your family into a great nation. I will go with you down to Egypt, and I will bring you back again. You will die in Egypt, but Joseph will be with you to close your eyes.” I will go with you. I will make you a great nation. I will bring you back. When you know that God is with you in the change, you can face it with so much more confidence. Someone who follows Jesus still has to deal with change, but they go through it with help. Jesus promises that “he will be with us, even to the very end of the age” as we seek to follow him and help others follow him. He will be with you at school, when you’re playing sports, hanging with your friends, all the time. He will not abandon you to handle change alone. King David knew that God would be with him through the hardest parts of life. He wrote in Psalm 23, "Even though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, I will fear no evil, for you are with me, your rod and your staff they comfort me." You may walk through the valley of the shadow of the teenage years, but God will guide you. You may walk through the valley of peer pressure, breaking up with a girl, hard classes, and conflict with others, but you won’t walk alone.
questions: Who in your family do you think handles change the best? Who do you think handles it worst? Why?
Have you ever sensed God's leadership, help, or nudges when going through a hard season of change? If so, what was it?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share if they have ever sensed God leading and guiding them through a season of change. What was the change, and how did they sense God's leadership?

thoughts.verse: So be strong and courageous! Do not be afraid and do not panic before them. For the LORD your God will personally go ahead of you. He will neither fail you nor abandon you.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God for his presence and guidance today in a situation or change you are facing. Ask him to speak in a way that matters to you and gives hope. Then listen for his voice throughout the day.

days.title: God Is Faithful In The Midst Of Change
days.scripture: GENESIS 48:1-12 1 One day not long after this, word came to Joseph, “Your father is failing rapidly.” So Joseph went to visit his father, and he took with him his two sons, Manasseh and Ephraim. 2 When Joseph arrived, Jacob was told, “Your son Joseph has come to see you.” So Jacob gathered his strength and sat up in his bed. 3 Jacob said to Joseph, “God Almighty appeared to me at Luz in the land of Canaan and blessed me. 4 He said to me, ‘I will make you fruitful, and I will multiply your descendants. I will make you a multitude of nations. And I will give this land of Canaan to your descendants after you as an everlasting possession.’ 5 “Now I am claiming as my own sons these two boys of yours, Ephraim and Manasseh, who were born here in the land of Egypt before I arrived. They will be my sons, just as Reuben and Simeon are. 6 But any children born to you in the future will be your own, and they will inherit land within the territories of their brothers Ephraim and Manasseh. 7 “Long ago, as I was returning from Paddan-aram, Rachel died in the land of Canaan. We were still on the way, some distance from Ephrath (that is, Bethlehem). So with great sorrow I buried her there beside the road to Ephrath.” 8 Then Jacob looked over at the two boys. “Are these your sons?” he asked. 9 “Yes,” Joseph told him, “these are the sons God has given me here in Egypt.” And Jacob said, “Bring them closer to me, so I can bless them.” 10 Jacob was half blind because of his age and could hardly see. So Joseph brought the boys close to him, and Jacob kissed and embraced them. 11 Then Jacob said to Joseph, “I never thought I would see your face again, but now God has let me see your children, too!” 12 Joseph moved the boys, who were at their grandfather’s knees, and he bowed with his face to the ground.
days.reflection: Lots of folks your age can get really anxious about change. It can cause them to lose sleep, make poor choices, feel overwhelmed and even depressed. It can feel like life is spiraling out of control, and you are powerless to deal with all the forces moving around you. In some sense that is natural, because the teenage years can be totally crazy. You experience so many things for the first time, and there is lots of pressure to not screw up and get life right. But if you are in a relationship with God, you can handle the pressure differently, because you know there is a purpose God is working out in all the change. You are not an accident; you are here for a reason. God likes you, and as you learned yesterday, he will be with you. His love is a steady source we can rely on in the midst of all the change. Below the surface of everything changing is an unchanging God. What happens around us may change, but God never changes. His love is steady, constant, and unfailing. He is outside of time, which means he is not affected by what happens in the same way we are. He doesn’t freak out, isn’t caught off guard, and isn’t taken by surprise. He is working day by day to get you where you need to be. Don’t lose heart even if things seem crazy. You can see this play out in Joseph's family. In this moving scene above, Jacob is reunited with Joseph and not only gets to see the son he thought was dead before he dies, but he gets to see his grandkids and bless them too! God had made a promise to Jacob that he would be blessed and be the father of a great nation, and now he is seeing it happen before his very eyes and before he dies. This promise is coming true. "I will make you fruitful, and I will multiply your descendants. I will make you a multitude of nations." In spite of all the impossible situations Jacob and Joseph have faced, God has been true to his word. His love is unchanging, his purposes unchanging, and their destiny unchanging, regardless of the chaos all around. If you commit yourself to the Lord and walk with him, he will be faithful to fulfill the promises of the gospel in your life too! He will be faithful through the ups and downs of your story. He will be a constant source of love and encouragement. 1 John 4:16 says something really cool: “We know how much God loves us, and we have put our trust in his love.” You can trust in God’s love for you. You can rely on it as fuel to deal with the drama of change. Though things will be hard, you will have a secret source to access. A confidence that life isn’t random. God has helped tons of teenagers through change in the bible. Joseph, Daniel, Jeremiah, Mary the mother of Jesus. They all learned to hope in God’s great love, and it got them through some intense times. Lamentations 3:22-27 has an amazing promise of walking with God through change. Fresh mercy every day. “The faithful love of the LORD never ends! His mercies never cease. Great is his faithfulness; his mercies begin afresh each morning. I say to myself, 'The LORD is my inheritance; therefore, I will hope in him!’” God will be faithful today. Ask him to show you in a tangible way.
questions: Your generation is the most anxious generation in history. What are some of the things that kids your age tend to worry about the most? What are you most stressed out about right now?

Warren W. Wiersbe says this: “Real contentment must come from within. You and I cannot change or control the world around us, but we can change and control the world within us.” How can God's love help us do this?
Ask the person you are doing this study with what things can tend to stress them out. How have they experienced God's love as a source of comfort and strength? Ask them to share a story about it.

thoughts.verse: The LORD is good to those who depend on him, to those who search for him. So it is good to wait quietly for salvation from the LORD. And it is good for people to submit at an early age to the yoke of his discipline.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to give God any anxiety you might be holding onto. List it all out and get it all off your chest. Then, thank God for his steady love and his faithfulness to stand beside you through every change you experience in life.

days.title: Embracing Change
days.scripture: GENESIS 47:27-28 27 Meanwhile, the people of Israel settled in the region of Goshen in Egypt. There they acquired property, and they were fruitful, and their population grew rapidly. 28 Jacob lived for seventeen years after his arrival in Egypt, so he lived 147 years in all.
days.reflection: So far you have been studying the life of Joseph for 9 weeks! Next week is the last week in the first shift along the Primal Path (from EASE to DIFFICULTY). You have seen the wild rollercoaster of a life Joseph has had and the ways that God has been faithful to him through it all. It may not have been the life he planned, but it’s been a good one. He has learned to overcome the difficulty of almost every conceivable kind of change. Change in relationships. Change in location. Change in position. Change of name. Change of family. Change of culture. Yet, he has learned to embrace everything that has happened and walk faithfully through it all. And hopefully, by now, you are starting to see that God will be there to walk you through whatever changes you face. Though some of them may make you anxious and some of them make you excited, you can know that God will ultimately work these things together for your good. God has the power to take even the craziest changes you face and work something cool out of them. And the circumstances around you don’t have to be perfect for this to happen either. Take a look at what happened in the scripture reading from today. While the rest of the world is experiencing a harsh famine, God has worked out for Joseph's family to be provided for and protected. “Meanwhile, the people of Israel settled in the region of Goshen in Egypt. There they acquired property, and they were fruitful, and their population grew rapidly.” God offers this same promise to his people again and again throughout history. Look at the contrast of what life is like trusting in God versus simply relying on the culture around you found in Jeremiah 17:5-8: “This is what the LORD says: ‘Cursed are those who put their trust in mere humans, who rely on human strength and turn their hearts away from the LORD. They are like stunted shrubs in the desert, with no hope for the future. They will live in the barren wilderness, in an uninhabited salty land. But blessed are those who trust in the LORD and have made the LORD their hope and confidence. They are like trees planted along a riverbank, with roots that reach deep into the water. Such trees are not bothered by the heat or worried by long months of drought. Their leaves stay green, and they never stop producing fruit.'" God has led Joseph's family through an amazing amount of change so they can flourish in the midst of the famine. And because they trust in him, they are experiencing provision in a time of lack. God is the one who has weaved an amazing story with twists and turns to get his people to this point where they will survive in the chaos. As a young man seeking to grow into a strong and godly man, try and take this deep into your heart. You do not have to fear change the way others do because God is working his purpose out for your life. He will give you hope when you struggle, direction when you are confused, power to persevere, and grit to grind it out. Rely on him. Look to his love. You can flourish in an anxious generation. You can enjoy his presence in times of confusion. You can face the future with confidence because God has good things in store for you. Just wait and see.
questions: Can you think of some ways you have seen God bless your family in the midst of all the hard things that have happened in our world these past few years? Can you list a couple?
Jeremiah listed out some strong promises for those who trust in the Lord. What do you think they would look like in a teenager’s life today?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share a time when they saw God provide in an amazing way in a really hard time. How did this give them the confidence to face changes in the future?

thoughts.verse: But forget all that—it is nothing compared to what I am going to do. For I am about to do something new. See, I have already begun! Do you not see it? I will make a pathway through the wilderness. I will create rivers in the dry wasteland.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God for confidence to face the future. Commit any big plans you have coming up to the Lord, and ask for his help and love to be with you.

extras: WEEK 9 MANSCHOOL CHANGE IT UP MANSCHOOL REMINDER: "The recipe for fun is pretty simple when you’re raising boys: add to any activity an element of danger, stir in a little exploration, add a dash of destruction, and you’ve got yourself a winner." John Eldredge OPTION 1. CHANGE IT UP Plan out an amazing night with your son. Food, activity, desserts. Get him involved in the details. Then, when you are on your way there, change the plans without telling him. Go somewhere else, do something else (make sure it’s something you know he will enjoy). Then on the way home discuss his response to change. What did he feel, how did he go through the 4 step process of change? 1-Shock and freaking out. 2-Anger and resistance. 3-Coming to terms with the "new normal." 4-Acceptance, adjusting, moving forward. OPTION 2. "HOW WOULD YOU HANDLE" DISCUSSION The Stoics have a practice called “the premeditation of evil.” The idea is to think about things that could go wrong in your life so that if they do happen, they don’t catch you off guard. You can take that same idea with change and turn it into a discussion. Get a big sheet of paper out on the table and list all the changes that will happen in the coming years for your son. Puberty. Dating. Breaking up with a girl. Learning to drive. Getting into an accident. Getting pulled over by the police. Failing a class. Being rejected or betrayed by a friend. Being shamed on social media. Not getting into the college you want. Giving into sin. Make this list as
FIELD NOTES PRAYER FOR MY SON
